sentences.text: Yes.
[LAUGHS] He had-- he had an offer.
He said, what, so long she's not coming?
I'm going to remarry.
I'm not waiting for her anymore.
So they come up one.
They said, you know what I have?
A beautiful girl, beautiful-looking one.
He said, who is she?
Oh, she has a mother and another sister.
He said, oh no.
I'm not taking three-- three womans on my shoulders.
No way.
So, I was lucky.
He didn't took it.
So I come, he took me.
Then when I was very sick, and he was traveling with his food, surrounding little towns around Vilna.
So he go in, in a town where-- used to his girlfriend used to live before the war.
So he said, oh, he go.
I go to look for her.
So when he's going to look, we went to the police.
And the police-- he went in the police.
He asked the policeman, you know, by the name such-and-such?
He said, I'm her husband.
So he said to him, he said to-- to Sam, who are you?
I said, I was a friend for your wife once.
Can I go and see her?
He said, all right.
Come on.
He took him to her.
But he couldn't stay with them too long.
Because he was in the police.
He had to go back to work.
So, she said to him, I know your wife is here.
And I have for you a suggestion.
Let's go away, both of us.
Leave your wife, and come with me.
So, I don't know.
My husband had maybe a little feeling.
I don't know what was it.
So he said, no.
I'm not going.
So I was a second time lucky.
He didn't want to go with nobody, just with me.
And then when I was healthy, come back home, and we staying-- we staying in Vilna till-- till we immigrate to Lodz.
I was liberated together with the girls from the little town, and with my cousin.
We come all together in Vilna.
And then they come, to come the soldiers, young Vilna soldiers.
They liberated from the Russian army back home.
So my cousin fell in love with the one from the Vilna soldier.
Another girl fall in love with one from the Vilna soldier.
I had my husband.
I stay with my husband.
So they all marry up, and on-- we was-- I left first Vilna.
They still were saying in Vilna.
And then-- then-- then there was going all of them went to Israel, from Poland-- from Vilna to Poland, from Poland to-- to Israel.
I was emigrated to Lodz.
And it was wintertime.
I was pregnant with my daughter.
And we didn't have any money.
We didn't have any clothes.
We had only one five rubles gold.
And this was 19-- in 1946.
And then I was going past the windows in Lodz.
There was food.
There was sweets.
There was cakes.
There was everything.
And I couldn't afford nothing, absolutely.
We had the $5, the 5 rubles gold.
What do we first?
First we need a piece of bread, and second we needed where to sleep.
We don't have-- just we was lucky.
We was-- then before the war, it was the organization, to Betar, me and my husband.
So we heard that there-- we were-- we come to them.
We told them, look, we have no place to sleep.
She said, don't worry.
Here, we have a place for you to sleep.
So they give us-- it was a house, a flat to live there.
Now this was so cold, freezing cold.
And then I said to him, look.
My teeth was hurting me from-- from the pregnancy.
So there was a cabinet-- I never forget this.
There was a cabinet with medicine there.
And he run to the medicine.
He said, this will be good.
This will be good.
If I would take, I would lose my pregnancy.
[LAUGHS] This I will never forget.
And we stayed there.
And then we stayed there about a couple months, from Betar.
They give us-- and we sold the $5 gold.
What my husband wanted, there was a time that everybody from Poland was handling with Russian, [NON-ENGLISH], and they're handling, make business.
They took from Poland, bring to Russia, from Russia and Poland.
Just it was in this time, there was divide Polish people.
They called [NON-ENGLISH].
If they even meet a Jewish person on the train, they took him out, and shoot him, after the war.
This was the Polacks.
So I said, no way.
I'd rather have hunger.
But you're not going.
To make everything through and go to lose it because of that, who cares?
He didn't went.
So we find, they help us a little bit with the food.
And then we-- we have to go from Poland to go to Germany to Berlin.
And this was a border.
You have to go, nobody could list you.
So the organization, Betar, they had the-- the cars-- not the cars, like big trucks.
They took us on the trucks, and very quietly we went through the border, without money.
Who has money has to pay.
But first, and then [INAUDIBLE].
And then we went to Berlin.
We come to Berlin.
We meet a lot of Vilna people.
And when he went out, of the streets come up a German to him, and said, look, I have such watch and a big, a big chain.
You want to buy it for bread?
So we had nothing, no money, no bread.
So he went into the-- to the camp.
There, there was-- and he said to one from Vilna, look, can you lend me something?
A-- a German waiting on the street with a watch.
And I-- I don't what it is.
Another one already know what it is.
So he took him, went with him, give him that, and I don't know, 5 mark, or whatever it is for the watch.
And he sold them.
He bring me a dress.
He bring me shoes.
He dress himself.
We had-- we had everything.
And we were sleeping in a bed like that.
It was a camp, just for all the people right after the war, such a small bed.
There we stay not for a long time, until he find his brother.
And we shift into-- to Kulmbach.
Berlin, it was-- it was summer.
It was in summer, in 1946.
I think that was already like in May, something like that.
This was warm in 1946 we left Berlin.
Oh, for a short term, it was maybe a month, I don't know if that.
And then we shift to Kulmbach.
And then he find his brother.
And we lived in Kulmbach in a private house.
There was a time you could come to the-- to the German government there, and tell them, I need a house.
So whatever you like it, from the Germany, you could take it.
And [INAUDIBLE] start in 1946.
So we took one of the best houses from the Germans.
And we tell him, before you was the boss.
Now we are the bosses.
You go down.
We go up.
There, but it was a very short time.
This straight away was changed.
It is-- Kulmbach is by Bamberg, by [NON-ENGLISH] and between these parts.
In Germany, yes.
And there we stayed till 1948, when Israel is liberated, with the Jewish states.
And we emigrate from there to Israel.
Nothing.
My husband was handling with cigarettes, with materials.
He was going from Kulmbach to Bamberg, to [INAUDIBLE], and he sold things like that.
And he made money there.
My daughter was born in-- in Germany.
When she was born, she was the first Jewish child.
Well, the German was thinking, she have two nose, two ears.
I don't know what they were thinking.
So she was a very big girl.
So I had a special nurse for her, from the hospital took her.
So she said to me, look, I take the girl in my-- she was sick.
She was in a hospital.
They give her-- in a German hospital they has to give them chocolate and cocoa.
We all get it from the UNRRA.
And we had it, but what a hospital.
They didn't give it to her.
They're eating themselves, and didn't give her nothing.
Still with the antisemitism was very bad after the war.
So he was-- he come there, and he said, what she still weigh the same when I bring her?
Doesn't it prove nothing?
So then he saw in a paper, in a German paper, the sister advertising, she want to look after her-- a child.
And we find her.
She was-- and she said-- her father was SS man.
Her mother was SS man.
She one from the big SS mens.
She says straight away.
But she said to us different.
If you want me, the child to bring to healthy-- I had my father-in-law.
He was crazy about my daughter.
Not one, not one can go to the child.
I take my responsibility.
And my father-in-law used to say through the hole from the key, he used to look-- look at them, to look at-- what-- if she's something-- do nothing to them-- to her.
And she brought her.
She brought her up, and she was very, very healthy.
When she was walking up the street, and the Germans want to come and look at her, how she looks.
Nobody could have a right to-- to look at her, how she looked.
Because right away, she's a very delicate child.
And she couldn't-- she couldn't-- she was afraid she catch up something from them.
And then after 1948, we immigrate to Israel.
Yes.
Ilsa Becker.
Ilsa Becker.
We was looking.
We went the first time that was 1967, to Europe for a trip.
We was looking for her.
We went to Dusseldorf, to Germany.
And we looked for her.
We couldn't find her.
She wanted to go with us to Israel.
When I was pregnant really with my daughter, and there was a doctor there in this hospital.
He said to me not to have any children, to make abortion.
There was an old woman living with me in-- in the same house in Vilna when I was pregnant.
And I come to her.
And I told her that I'm pregnant.
And I don't want to make abortion.
I don't want to live without children.
And I said, look, who knows what will be?
And she said to me, the old fashioned, she said, when you're pregnant, everything goes up.
Your lungs will-- will-- will close it up.
You'll be able to.
Don't listen to the doctor.
Yes, it was for a time.
I couldn't get, yes, it was a time.
Since I-- since I went up in my weight, and I was looking all right, everything come to normal.
Before, yes.
From Kulmbach we went to Israel.
That was in 1948.
I think that was maybe like in-- April, something like that, or it was in May-- something like this.
But it was warm in Germany.
It was warm.
Short time after Israel's independence, short time, very short time.
By ship.
We went to really for me and my daughter and my husband, we wasn't on the paper to go to Israel.
Just his whole family, the brothers, and the sister-in-laws, and the father, they all was ready to go to Israel.
So we didn't-- we didn't-- we wasn't on the list.
So my husband went to [INAUDIBLE] there.
And said to them, what you done?
My whole family is going to-- to Israel, and you didn't put me in.
Shush, shush, shush, shush, don't-- now be nervous.
We put you-- we put you in.
And then we went by ship.
It was we went to Marseilles, went to Paris, the French there.
We stopped there.
And from there, we went to-- to Haifa.
We was in, that was in town here, there was a camp, for all coming new-comer.
And we went into this camp.
They bring us there by camp.
We're staying in the camp.
There was a brochure in Yiddish.
And the brochure was written [YIDDISH]-- and in-- what do you call in like-- I cannot talk.
There was written [YIDDISH] and the side was written Drabkin.
On [YIDDISH] there was Drabkin.
So my husband went up to the front and said, look, we have here a family, Drabkin.
It's spelled exactly-- because you have two Drabkins.
One is spelled with a B is the family.
One with a P has nothing to do.
So he said, look, we have a prime minister in the government here a Drabkin.
So we call him up.
He said, this where our family address.
They went to bring a [INAUDIBLE].
It was alive to him in the parliament.
And I said to him, there was a policeman in front.
We said to him, well, where are you?
Who are you?
He said, we are [INAUDIBLE] family, the Drabkins.
So we went.
He went out.
He went out to you, and looked at them.
And he said, all right, where are you?
He said, I'm in camp in Netanya.
So next time he come with a black limousine to the-- to the whole family.
And he asked, what-- what you needed?
What you want?
He said, we want to have a-- a roof over our head.
So everyone get our little-- our [? apparels. ?] There was nothing to leave there.
But we went away.
It was all right.
And how long did you stay in the first place, the camp?
In the camp, we stay for a couple of months.
And then we went to Jaffa.
That's the house which we get it.
And there was one room, Arab room.
There was nothing there, absolutely nothing, just a room.
And my husband has two golden hands.
So he had to make another room, he make a toilet, he make a shower, he built-- built completely our own apartment, a two-bed-- two bed-- a one-bedroom apartment.
So it was a lounge, and there was like a bedroom, and a little kitchen, a shower, and a toilet.
It was something out-- out of the world.
It was something beautiful.
And-- and then we was living there about a couple years.
And then we were shifting in a new home.
And when we're staying one year, in the new home, my cousin from Canada come visit us.
And she said to us, no.
You have-- you have such two golden hands.
You're sitting here?
Come to Canada.
You make a fortune.
She said, I'm going back.
And I'm going to make some papers for you.
You sell the flat.
And we had 10 [INAUDIBLE], so one from the richest people in Israel.
I had my own apartment.
I had a fridge, when nobody has only of-- of ice to keep.
I had a fridge.
I have built furniture.
I had a washing machine.
I had everything.
It was so good, I never want to go out.
Anyway, I didn't want to go out from Vilna when I did nothing.
So from-- from Israel, my husband wanted to go.
He was all the time in the army then, every four weeks, they schlepped him that.
He said, I went through so much now.
All right, I can go to Canada.
He's easy.
I'll go.
So we sold everything, and we were sitting in-- in [INAUDIBLE], with my cousin in a room.
We're waiting for the papers.
Suddenly come a letter it's [INAUDIBLE] make agreement with Canada that doesn't let in Israelis.
So we stay with nothing.
We have not a house.
He has notice his-- his work, he was working, nothing.
So he was thinking.
Wait, I have a cousin in Australia.
I write to him a letter.
He write a letter.
Take a month, he sends the visa to come.
In the meantime, it was 1956.
Break-- break of the Sinai, the Sinai War.
So they took him.
Till I saw him back, when he come.
Then he said, whatever it is.
I'm going out.
We have no connection to going to Melbourne.
There was the Olympic games.
So we're going into [INAUDIBLE].
We had some money.
We went into [INAUDIBLE].
And we go into a Jewish restaurant.
And he ask him, look, do you have a place?
He said, yes, I have a boarding house.
You can stay at my place.
So he stayed there for a months.
And this was winter, very cold.
I said, look, with two small children, you have something to warm up?
He said, go in the station.
And the station is very warm.
You can warm up there yourself with the kids.
And my husband didn't have any-- he couldn't sit in one place.
We had some money.
So he went to the [INAUDIBLE].
He bought some gold for $2,000, gold he brought up.
He said, with this we're going to Israel.
And when we come from-- from the-- from-- to Australia, and when we come to Australia-- to Australia, and we have to go the-- the migration office.
He asked him, what do you have?
He said, what I have?
I have nothing.
I have a wife with two children.
Didn't you bring-- nothing, he said I bring nothing.
That's what I have.
He said, you went to the concentration camp through?
He said, yes.
Because you don't-- you don't talk to me normal.
What do you mean, you didn't bring nothing.
He said, I told you this.
I have-- you fine dress, we would come poor like anything.
So did you pay for your tickets to come from Israel to here?
Oh yeah.
Yeah, I pay for my ticket.
We were flying by plane.
My husband, my daughter, my son.
My son was born in Israel in 1952.
The 1st of May.
And my daughter was born in Germany, in Kulmbach, the-- the 5th-- the 6th 1946.
Pauline.
Aric.
I have five grandchildren.
I have two from my late daughter.
The one is Charlie.
He's 25 years old, and another one from my late daughter who's 22, Darren.
And the other three is from my son.
One is Jessica.
And she's 13 years old.
And the twins, a brother and a sister, will be delivered next month.
How affected-- what-- when I went through what I went through in the concentration camp, it was-- it was affect me, because I miss my parents.
I miss-- but I didn't think so much.
But I hung on what I have.
At the time, when I went through, I didn't think about it so much.
I just was thinking one thing.
I have to find my husband.
That's all what was on my mind, nothing else.
Well, nobody would believe it, but that was it.
Yes, straight away.
I was-- all of my friends and my cousin, they all was learning to sewing on a machine.
And not on a machine what used to be on the older time with your foot, but there was a motor.
Like that, you know, the machines with the hand motor.
And I couldn't sew.
I know how to sewing, but not such a-- where it has to run.
Quick done, and otherwise, you couldn't make the money.
So I was-- I was-- I went to my cousin.
I said to her, look, I-- I could learn to-- to sew on my machine.
And my husband will buy separate, and he will make the machine together.
Don't worry. she said, look I couldn't give you my boss.
So I find one, what give me work.
And I was sewing like this-- like the slacks.
And he used to pay us three shillings or $0.06 a pair.
It was a lot of money.
But I was so slow.
I make one pair one day, and all my friends-- when they were sewing, they used to make 25 pound a week.
I said, oh no.
I'm not such a schlimazel I'm going to learn.
I was very ambitious.
And I learned.
They were-- all of them, they was afraid I will take away the bosses from them.
I used to earn 50 pound a week, so quick I was.
The boss said to me when I left him, he said to me, I never saw such a machinist like you was.
The machine was too slow for me, so quick I was.
And that's what-- then I was-- I wasn't working a long time, maybe a year, by the machine.
Then I-- we had a sandwich bar.
I worked together with my husband.
19 and 1/2 years.
[LAUGHS] Yeah.
I retired in 1976.
That was the first time with Sam and I, we make a trip over [INAUDIBLE].
I was back already 18 times in Israel, every year practically.
Yes, twice I've been to Vilna.
Once I went to Vilna.
There was the Russian.
So we have to fly to London, to pick up the paper there.
To go with a Russian plane, there was no such a thing like a Russian plane then, maybe now.
And we went, we get the papers.
And we had two-- like three days in Moscow, three days in Vilna, three days in Riga, and three days in Leningrad.
So we come to Moscow, when it was the Russian.
And when you come to Moscow, you have to declare how much money you have, what rings you have, jewelry what you wear, everything has to be declared there.
So we declared, and we went into Moscow, and we paid here in England.
Everything was paid, for luxury hotels in Moscow.
Everything perfect.
And we were still afraid, whatever it was.
Then when we went in, we landed in-- in Moscow, and we meet two couples, one was from Canada, a Jewish couple, one was from England, no Jewish.
And we all six keep the two weeks together.
We so close to each other, that I went last year.
I visit them in England.
And nine-- and five years ago, we went to visit them in England, and we make a tour in Scotland.
So we have to wait six days.
So we ring them up.
They left us the key.
We went to the brother, [INAUDIBLE].
We was very close with them.
And they keep us there for a week.
We went with them.
Till now, we're still in contact.
And did you go to Vilna?
I went to Vilna.
I went three days in Moscow.
And from Moscow, we went to Vilna.
We find we was-- really we took a taxi by ourselves, with one.
We was traveling around Vilna.
At first we went to Ponary.
This was the first place, where there was buried my mother and his mother.
And the couples went with us together.
The two couples, what-- they was interested to see everything.
And there was in the Ponary there was written there was buried here 100,000 Russian citizens.
There was nothing about Jewish.
The second time when I come to Vilna, there was no Russia anymore.
There is a big monument was written that here was buried 75,000 Vilna Jewish people.
In the town, I found my place where-- where I was living, his not.
His place is ruined.
It's-- It's different.
Because it's every-- every street you're going, you-- you were brought up there all your life, you know.
And you can't dare, you don't find nobody.
It's nothing there.
It's just an emptiness there, just empty.
It's painful, very painful to go there.
The second time when I went to Vilna, I-- I go into my home where I was living.
And there was a Russian woman, a Russian family lived there.
And I opened the door, and I said, can I go in?
I was living here with my mother, and father, and brother, and sister.
And she-- she said, yes.
Come in.
I went in.
Suddenly, I couldn't recognize the place.
But it was hurting, this was very hurt.
And I went out.
I left her $10, American $10.
Oh, she didn't know what to do with us.
You want a cup of tea?
Because the wages was $20 a month.
And she-- she said-- she was so-- so really friendly.
And then I went to the place what I was learning, to my school.
When-- when we had the business there, there is nothing there, nothing there.
The theater that I used to go is there, it's still there.
A lot of places what you still can recognize.
But these were built-- out of Vilna is all high-rising flats.
It used to be all the little houses, you know?
That's what the character with Vilna was.
Now it's-- it's completely changed.
The old city, the old city is still there.
So when you're going by car, you have to pay 50 [NON-ENGLISH] there for-- for Russian money, for to go into-- to the old city, you know?
Because most of them is in new city, everything built up.
And did you see any people that you knew from before?
We went-- no, I didn't see the people from before.
We went in.
There was still one synagogue left in Vilna.
So we went in, and we find there the shamus, was provided with-- and the synagogue was rebuilt.
And he told us, there is-- 8,000 or 8,000 Jews still in Vilna, not Vilna.
But from around Vilna, from Lithuania.
And a lot of them, they was-- immigrate to-- to Israel.
And then we wanted to go-- to go in another place, where there was a-- there was only back yard from the shul.
The shul was closed.
There was a goya there, a Polish woman there.
And she said, look, you can't go in.
Because it's closed, the shul.
So then we had a-- with a car, we went, and it was on the Shabbos.
There was-- have them prayer there.
So we go in there, and then we find some people what we know, not much.
